metadata.career_relevance: Flipkart's product list query with reviews was running 201 database queries for 200 products (1 for products + 200 for reviews). DataLoader collapsed it to 2 queries: 1 for products, 1 for all reviews (WHERE product_id IN (...)). At 10,000 QPS, this reduced DB load by 99.5%. After this topic, you can diagnose and fix the most common GraphQL performance bug.

hook.real_world_problem:
Flipkart product list page N+1 problem:

```
GraphQL query:
  { products { id name reviews { rating } } }

With @SchemaMapping (NAIVE):
  Query 1: SELECT * FROM products              (returns 200 products)
  Query 2: SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P1'
  Query 3: SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P2'
  ... (one per product)
  Query 201: SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id = 'P200'

  Total: 201 DB queries, 2.5 seconds

With @BatchMapping (DataLoader):
  Query 1: SELECT * FROM products              (returns 200 products)
  Query 2: SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id IN ('P1','P2',...,'P200')

  Total: 2 DB queries, 80ms
  Improvement: 100× faster, 99% fewer queries
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Flipkart product reviews BatchMapping that collapses 200 DB queries into 1.

visual_aid.description: Two diagrams. Left (N+1, naive): 200 product boxes each with their own arrow going down to the DB (200 arrows = 200 queries). Right (DataLoader/BatchMapping): 200 products collected into one batch, single arrow to DB with 'IN (P1, P2... P200)', results grouped and distributed back. Performance numbers: N+1: 201 queries, 2500ms. DataLoader: 2 queries, 80ms.
visual_aid.alt_text: N+1: one DB call per product. DataLoader: one DB call for all products.

worked_example.scenario: Flipkart product reviews: @BatchMapping to eliminate N+1.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Add @BatchMapping reviews(List<Product>) to existing controller
2. ReviewRepository: findByProductIdIn(List<String>)
3. Group results by productId → return Map<Product, List<Review>>
4. @SpringBootTest: verify ONE query executed for multiple products

step_by_step_solution.details: DataLoader caches results within a single GraphQL request. If two parts of a query both request the same product's reviews, DataLoader calls the batch function once and serves the cached result to both. This is request-scoped (cleared between requests) — no stale data risk.

things_to_notice: @BatchMapping return type: Map<ParentType, ChildType>. The key is the parent object (Product), the value is what the field should return (List<Review>). Spring uses this map to match each product to its reviews.
@BatchMapping replaces @SchemaMapping entirely — don't use both for the same field. One or the other.
Missing products in the map: if a product has no reviews, include it with an empty list (not missing from the map). If a product is missing from the map, Spring propagates null for that field.
DataLoader works even for single-item queries: { product(id: P1) { reviews { } } }. Even for one product, DataLoader still batches (though it's trivial — 1 item in the batch).

industry_examples.use_case: Merchant dashboard: 50 payments shown with their merchant details. @SchemaMapping merchant() called 50 times → 50 queries. @BatchMapping merchant(List<Payment>) → 1 query: SELECT * FROM merchants WHERE id IN (50 merchant IDs). Response time: 800ms → 45ms. Same pattern for payment.refunds, payment.dispute.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you solve the N+1 problem in GraphQL?' → DataLoader / @BatchMapping. Instead of @SchemaMapping calling DB once per parent (N times), @BatchMapping receives all parents as a list, executes ONE batch query (SELECT * FROM table WHERE id IN (...)), groups results by parent, returns Map<Parent, Child>. Spring for GraphQL: @BatchMapping annotation handles the DataLoader machinery automatically.
applications.real_world_workflow: DataLoader adoption: (1) identify slow queries in logs (repeated similar queries = N+1), (2) replace @SchemaMapping with @BatchMapping, (3) add JPA repository method: findByXxx idIn(List<String> ids), (4) group results: Collectors.groupingBy, (5) return Map<ParentType, List<ChildType>>, (6) verify: query count drops from N+1 to 2.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Returning List<Review> instead of Map<Product, List<Review>> from @BatchMapping. Spring can't map reviews back to the correct product.
common_gaps.remediation: BatchMapping must return Map<ParentType, ReturnType>. The key is the parent object (Product), not the parent ID. Spring uses reference equality to match. Alternative: use Map<String, List<Review>> (key = productId String) if you prefer String keys — but the Map key must match what Spring can correlate.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: @BatchMapping method returns List<Review> instead of Map<Product, List<Review>> — compilation error or ClassCastException at runtime

key_takeaways: N+1: @SchemaMapping called N times for N parents = N+1 DB queries total
@BatchMapping: called ONCE with ALL parents → one batch query → Map<Parent, Child>
Repository: add findByIdIn(List<String>) for the batch query
Map return: key=parent object, value=child list (products with no reviews → empty list, not missing)
DataLoader cache: within one request, same key = cached (no duplicate batch calls)
@BatchMapping replaces @SchemaMapping — don't use both for the same field
Performance: N+1 → 2 queries (100× improvement for large lists)

self_check_questions.question: A Swiggy query returns 30 restaurants, each with their menu (items list). How many DB queries are made without DataLoader vs with DataLoader?
self_check_questions.answer: Without DataLoader (@SchemaMapping): 31 queries. 1 query for 30 restaurants + 1 menu query per restaurant = 30 menu queries. Total: 31. With DataLoader (@BatchMapping): 2 queries. 1 query for 30 restaurants + 1 batch query 'SELECT * FROM menus WHERE restaurant_id IN (R1, R2, ..., R30)'. Total: 2. At 500 concurrent users each loading 30 restaurants: Without: 500 × 31 = 15,500 DB queries/second. With DataLoader: 500 × 2 = 1,000 DB queries/second. DataLoader is not optional for production GraphQL with nested data.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: GraphQL Subscriptions — real-time updates via WebSocket. Track live stock prices on Zerodha or order status changes on Swiggy.
